title: Polyglot Dev Atlas home page
description: Landing page for the Polyglot Dev Atlas, showcasing cross-language code patterns and insights.

polyglot_dev_atlas.title: Polyglot Dev Atlas
polyglot_dev_atlas.text:
This atlas helps compare practical code patterns across C++, Python, Go, TypeScript, and Scala.

Quick Usage Guide:
1. Choose one or two languages from the LANGUAGES row.
2. Click ATLAS VIEWS / LEARNING VIEWS (the colored row title) to switch the whole view set.
3. Pick a view chip (for example Keywords, Workflow, Exercises, Interview).
4. Use the item dropdown (Problem, Question, Task) to select an entry.
5. Toggle Compare 2 to see side-by-side code.
6. Use Swap to switch left/right compared languages.
7. Use palette and theme controls to adjust style.
8. In Exercises, click Show Solution to reveal code per-language panels.

What Each View Set Contains: ATLAS VIEWS (Keywords, Workflow, Design Patterns, Principles) and LEARNING VIEWS (Lang. Basic, Course, Exercises, Problems, Interview).

Practical Tips: Start with Keywords for quick syntax recall; use Problems or Interview for realistic patterns; use Exercises for guided practice; keep compare mode on when mapping concepts across languages.

cross_language_advanced_patterns.title: Cross-language Advanced Patterns
cross_language_advanced_patterns.description: High-level mapping of advanced patterns across languages
cross_language_advanced_patterns.text: Legend: ✗ means no close built-in equivalent. 'Partial (approx)' means conceptually similar but semantics differ.

rows: template<class T> T max2(T a,T b)
func(x int) int { return x + bias }
ranges::copy_if(v, out, p); ranges::transform(out, out2, f)
[f(x) for x in xs if p(x)]
v, err := fn(); if err != nil { ... }
Partial (approx):Result<T,E> object with ok/value or ok/error branches
implicits or givens plus type classes

practical_orientation.title: Practical Orientation
practical_orientation.description: Short operational notes and developer pointers
practical_orientation.text: For maintainers, see DEVELOPMENT.md for content contracts, validation, and build-check workflows. Use this page as orientation, then move into ATLAS VIEWS or LEARNING VIEWS for side-by-side code exploration.

graphical_usage_guide.title: Graphical Usage Guide
graphical_usage_guide.description: Visual guidance and interaction notes for the atlas UI
graphical_usage_guide.text:
The interactive guidance on this site is implemented using CSS and JS (no bundled animation files). Key UX points:

- LANGUAGES row: toggles which language columns are shown.
- ATLAS/LEARNING view selector: switches sets of views (Keywords, Workflow, Exercises, etc.).
- Compare mode: enables side-by-side panels for two languages; use Swap to flip sides.
- Show Solution: reveals per-language code panels in Exercises.

For maintainers: add CSS animations in the UI layer (e.g., keyframes for fades, transforms for swaps). Avoid embedding large media in content JSON; prefer CSS classes that the renderer maps to animations.

language_specific_features.title: Language-specific Features
language_specific_features.description: Concise highlights for each supported language to help orient users to idiomatic differences.

cpp: Access control nuances (`protected`, friend declarations, inheritance visibility)
RAII and deterministic destruction for resource safety
Move semantics (`T&&`, move constructors, perfect forwarding)
Allocator-aware containers and custom memory ownership models
python: Decorators for wrapping and registering behavior
Runtime introspection and monkey patching patterns
go: Channels with select-based coordination and cancellation
`defer` for scoped cleanup and post-return hooks
Implicit interface satisfaction and composition-first APIs
Error wrapping (`%w`) and idiomatic sentinel handling
typescript: Conditional and distributive types for type-level branching
Mapped types and key remapping over object structures
Template literal types for string-shape constraints
Control-flow narrowing with custom type guards
scala: Effect-style composition with monads and for-comprehensions

cpp.intro: C++ gives you direct control over memory and object lifetime. That makes performance strong, but it also means ownership rules matter from day one.
mindsets: Think in terms of object "lifetime": stack, heap, and who owns cleanup.
Prefer value semantics first, then add pointers when sharing or polymorphism is needed.
Use RAII wrappers so cleanup happens automatically.

snippets.title: RAII cleanup (automatic)
snippets.note: When scope ends, destructors run. No manual close call needed.

snippets.title: Move semantics (transfer ownership)
snippets.note: Use "std"::move to transfer expensive resources instead of copying.

python.intro: Python is optimized for readability and quick iteration. Many advanced patterns are runtime-based, so you can build power incrementally.
mindsets: Start with clear functions and classes before advanced magic methods.
Use decorators for cross-cutting behavior like logging or timing.
Think in "protocols": if it behaves like the needed interface, it usually works.

snippets.title: Decorator for reusable behavior
snippets.note: Wrap a function once, then reuse that behavior everywhere.

snippets.title: Context manager style cleanup
snippets.note: Use with to guarantee cleanup even when errors happen.
snippets.code:
with open("notes.txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") as "f":
    text = f.read()
# file is closed automatically

go.intro: Go keeps the language small and predictable. Concurrency and explicit error handling are core habits, not optional extras.
mindsets: Return errors as values and handle them immediately.
Use goroutines for concurrent work and channels for coordination.
Use defer right after acquiring a resource.

snippets.title: Error-first flow
snippets.note: Go favors explicit branches over exceptions.

snippets.title: Goroutine + channel
snippets.note: Start work concurrently and receive the result through a channel.

typescript.intro: TypeScript adds a strong static type layer on top of JavaScript. It is excellent for making APIs self-documenting and safer to refactor.
mindsets: Use union types to model possible states clearly.
Narrow types with guards before using specific fields.
Let interfaces describe behavior, then implement with any matching shape.

snippets.title: Discriminated union
snippets.note: Model variants and handle each one safely in switch.

snippets.title: Type guard narrowing
snippets.note: A guard helps TypeScript infer the precise type.

scala.intro: Scala blends object-oriented and functional styles. You can start simple, then gradually adopt stronger abstractions like Options, Eithers, and type classes.
mindsets: Prefer immutable collections and expression-style code.
Use Option instead of null for absent values.
Treat pattern matching as a primary control-flow tool.

snippets.title: Option instead of null
snippets.note: Represent missing values explicitly and handle both cases.
snippets.code:
val "maybeAge": Option[Int] = Some(20)
val label = maybeAge match
  case Some(age) => s"age=$age"
  case None => "age=unknown"

snippets.title: Collection pipeline
snippets.note: Transform data with small composable steps.
snippets.code:
val nums = List(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
val result = nums
  .filter(_ % 2 == 1)
  .map(_ * 10)
  .sum
